edge: The TUSLT model involves jointly learning a mapping network to infer the latent transformation, supporting simultaneous manipulation on multiple attributes.

The mapping network and the jointly trained classifier work together to extract domain-specific information to enhance attribute characterization.

The first stage of the architecture involves a direction prediction subnetwork, while the second stage incorporates a fusion subnetwork to operate on the produced directions.
The direction prediction subnetwork produces latent directions that are denoted by Γ(w), with each direction associated with a preset attribute conditioned on w.
The fusion subnetwork Φ integrates the produced directions indicated by ∆ and infers a residual vector γ∆w based on the target attributes.
The binary vector ∆ indicates the target attributes to be used in integration with the produced directions by the fusion subnetwork Φ.
The generator G decodes the resulting latent vector as ˆx∆ by adding the manipulation strength controlled by α to the residual vector γ∆w.
Each stage of the two-stage architecture has access to the latent code of the input image to ensure proper manipulation and generation.

For AnimeFace, we use the same setting as the experiments on CelebA-HQ. The results shown in Figures 9-10 confirm again that our model is scalable to multi-attribute transformations on animal and anime facial images and lead to significant visual modification.
We propose a text-guided unsupervised multi-attribute manipulation model to edit images in a single latent transformation.
The text-guided unsupervised multi-attribute manipulation model is proposed to edit images in a single latent transformation step.
Benefiting from the cross-modal image and text representation of CLIP, the model can jointly train an auxiliary attribute classifier and a latent mapping network for precise attribute manipulation.
The proposed model is able to successfully manipulate multiple attributes on diverse input images, compared to the leading methods.
The use of a two-stage architecture enables the model to take care of all preset individual attributes, providing the flexibility to handle diverse attribute combinations.
In contrast to CLIP encoders, the classifier learns domain-specific features to identify preset attributes.
The model offers semantic guidance not only for manipulating target attributes but for preserving irrelevant attributes of the input image.
This work significantly increases the scalability of StyleGAN-based image attribute manipulation without causing any manual annotation cost.
